persona: Fast-talking Manchester Depop seller obsessed with Y2K nostalgia and streetwear hype, always chasing the next viral archive drop. Speaks in quick, slang-heavy bursts and wants stock she can flip within days, not weeks.
salesNotes: Graphic tees and baby tees sell within 48 hours if priced £8-£15 buy-in, resold £20-£35; anything over £18 buy-in sits for weeks and gets discounted. Cargo trousers move fast in sizes 8-12 but slow above size 14. Branded zip-ups do well only if logo is clean and era-correct (2000s), faded/damaged ones sit dead stock. Avoid plain basics with no logo - dead weight in the last 6 months.

persona: Considered, slow-fashion Brighton shop owner who talks like a curator, valuing provenance, fabric weight, and repairability over trends. Prefers a smaller, well-chosen edit to a rack stuffed with filler.
salesNotes: Chore coats and waxed jackets in French/British brands (Le Laboureur, Barbour) consistently sell within 2 weeks at £70-£120, strong repeat customer base. Canvas trousers sell steadily but slower, roughly 3-4 weeks average, priced £45-£65. Wool jumpers with moth damage or synthetic blends sit unsold for months - only pure wool moves. Boots sell well but only larger sizes (9-11) shift quickly, smaller sizes languish.

persona: We're a London menswear vintage store curating clean, well-made pieces for guys who care about quality over quantity. Our buyers are stylists and collectors who'd rather pay a bit more for a mint Levi's trucker jacket than dig through a bargain bin of tatty stock.
salesNotes: Last 6 months: Levi's and Wrangler denim jackets in grade A/B sell within days at £45-£75, especially trucker styles; anything with heavy fading or holes sits for weeks even discounted. Leather biker and bomber jackets move fast at £60-£110 if clean, but cracked or stiff leather is dead stock. Workwear (Carhartt, Dickies, vintage chore coats) is our strongest riser - clean examples at £40-£65 sell same week. Boots (Red Wing, Dr Martens) move well at £50-£90 if soles/uppers are solid; scuffed or resoled pairs get marked down 30% and still linger. Overall trend: customers will pay a premium for curated, clean-grade pieces but ignore anything needing repair work, even at low prices.
